does: This paper introduces PRInTS, a generative process reward model designed to guide language model agents through long-horizon information-seeking tasks. Unlike existing process reward models that provide binary judgments, PRInTS generates dense scores across multiple dimensions of step quality, including interpretation of tool outputs and informativeness of tool calls. The model incorporates trajectory summarization capabilities that compress growing context while preserving information essential for evaluating subsequent steps. The authors evaluate PRInTS across three information-seeking benchmarks (FRAMES, GAIA levels 1-3, and WebWalkerQA) and demonstrate improvements in guiding agent performance on tasks requiring multi-step tool interaction and reasoning.
connects: This work extends the process reward model paradigm, which emerged as a way to improve reasoning in language models by scoring intermediate steps rather than only final outputs. Prior work on reward modeling for reasoning tasks focused primarily on mathematical or logical reasoning with relatively short trajectories and simple judgment dimensions. PRInTS responds to the specific challenge of information-seeking, where agents must interact with external tools and reason over their outputs—a problem highlighted by benchmarks like GAIA and WebWalkerQA. The paper builds on insights from research on context length limitations in transformers and connects to work on trajectory optimization and step-level feedback in agent learning. It combines ideas from process supervision with text summarization techniques to handle the expanding context that long-horizon tasks create.
enables: This work opens directions for more sophisticated agent guidance in open-ended information-seeking tasks that require extended interaction with external tools and knowledge sources. The dual-capability design of PRInTS—dense multi-dimensional scoring plus context compression—establishes a template for scaling reward modeling beyond short reasoning tasks. Future work could extend this approach to even longer horizons, incorporate learned summarization strategies tailored to specific domains, or use the dense dimensional scores for more fine-grained learning signals in agent training. The trajectory summarization component could also enable more efficient scaling of reward models to domains with very long planning horizons, potentially supporting agents tackling complex research tasks, multi-step planning problems, or interactive information retrieval systems that require sustained tool use.
